1	# Part 8: Followers
2	
3	> 来源: [https://blog.miguelgrinberg.com/post/the-flask-mega-tutorial-part-viii-followers](https://blog.miguelgrinberg.com/post/the-flask-mega-tutorial-part-viii-followers) | Flask Mega-Tutorial by Miguel Grinberg
4	
5	---
6	
7	This is the eighth installment of the Flask Mega-Tutorial series, in which I'm going to tell you how to implement a "followers" feature similar to that of Twitter and other social networks.
8	
9	You are reading the 2024 edition of the Flask Mega-Tutorial. The complete course is also available to order in e-book and paperback formats from Amazon. Thank you for your support!
10	
11	If you are looking for the 2018 edition of this course, you can find it here.
12	
13	For your reference, here is the complete list of articles in this series:
14	
15	- Chapter 1:  Hello, World!
16	
17	- Chapter 2:  Templates
18	
19	- Chapter 3:  Web Forms
20	
21	- Chapter 4:  Database
22	
23	- Chapter 5:  User Logins
24	
25	- Chapter 6:  Profile Page and Avatars
26	
27	- Chapter 7:  Error Handling
28	
29	- Chapter 8:  Followers (this article)
30	
31	- Chapter 9:  Pagination
32	
33	- Chapter 10:  Email Support
34	
35	- Chapter 11:  Facelift
36	
37	- Chapter 12:  Dates and Times
38	
39	- Chapter 13:  I18n and L10n
40	
41	- Chapter 14:  Ajax
42	
43	- Chapter 15:  A Better Application Structure
44	
45	- Chapter 16:  Full-Text Search
46	
47	- Chapter 17:  Deployment on Linux
48	
49	- Chapter 18:  Deployment on Heroku
50	
51	- Chapter 19:  Deployment on Docker Containers
52	
53	- Chapter 20:  Some JavaScript Magic
54	
55	- Chapter 21:  User Notifications
56	
57	- Chapter 22:  Background Jobs
58	
59	- Chapter 23:  Application Programming Interfaces (APIs)
60	
61	In this chapter I am going to work on the application's database some more. I want users of the application to be able to easily choose which other users they want to follow. So I'm going to be expanding the database to keep track of who is following who, which is trickier than you may think.
62	
63	*The GitHub links for this chapter are: Browse, Zip, Diff.*
64	
65	## Database Relationships Revisited
66	
67	I said above that I want to maintain a list of "followed" and "follower" users for each user. Unfortunately, a relational database does not have a list type that I can use for these lists, all there is are tables with records and relationships between these records.
68	
69	The database has a table that represents users, so what's needed is to come up with the proper relationship type that can model the follower/followed link. This is a good time to review the basic database relationship types:
70	
71	### One-to-Many
72	
73	I have already used a one-to-many relationship in Chapter 4. Here is the diagram for this relationship:
74	
75	The two entities linked by this relationship are users and posts. I say that a user has *many* posts, and a post has *one* user (or author). The relationship is represented in the database with the use of a *foreign key* on the "many" side. In the relationship above, the foreign key is the `user_id` field added to the `posts` table. This field links each post to the record of its author in the user table.
76	
77	It is pretty clear that the `user_id` field provides direct access to the author of a given post, but what about the reverse direction? For the relationship to be useful I should be able to get the list of posts written by a given user. The `user_id` field in the `posts` table is also sufficient to answer this question, as this column was given an index, to allow for efficient queries such us "retrieve all posts that have a user_id of X".
78	
79	### Many-to-Many
80	
81	A many-to-many relationship is a bit more complex. As an example, consider a database that has `students` and `teachers`. I can say that a student has *many* teachers, and a teacher has *many* students. It's like two overlapped one-to-many relationships from both ends.
82	
83	For a relationship of this type I should be able to query the database and obtain the list of teachers that teach a given student, and the list of students in a teacher's class. This is actually non-trivial to represent in a relational database, as it cannot be done by adding foreign keys to the existing tables.
84	
85	The representation of a many-to-many relationship requires the use of an auxiliary table called an *association table*. Here is how the database would look for the students and teachers example:
86	
87	While it may not seem obvious at first, the association table with its two foreign keys is able to efficiently answer all the queries about the relationship.
88	
89	### Many-to-One and One-to-One
90	
91	A many-to-one relationship is similar to a one-to-many relationship. The difference is that this relationship is looked at from the "many" side.
92	
93	A one-to-one relationship is a special case of a one-to-many. The representation is similar, but a constraint is added to the database to prevent the "many" side from having more than one link. While there are cases in which this type of relationship is useful, it isn't as common as the other types.
94	
95	## Representing Followers
96	
97	Looking at the summary of all the relationship types, it is easy to determine that the proper data model to track followers is the many-to-many relationship, because a user follows *many* users, and a user has *many* followers. But there is a twist. In the students and teachers example I had two entities that were related through the many-to-many relationship. But in the case of followers, I have users following other users, so there are just users. So what is the second entity of the many-to-many relationship?
98	
99	The second entity of the relationship is also the users. A relationship in which instances of a class are linked to other instances of the same class is called a *self-referential relationship*, and that is exactly what I have here.
100	
101	Here is a diagram of the self-referential many-to-many relationship that keeps track of followers:
102	
103	The `followers` table is the association table of the relationship. The foreign keys in this table are both pointing at entries in the user table, since it is linking users to users. Each record in this table represents one link between a follower user and a followed user. Like the students and teachers example, a setup like this one allows the database to answer all the questions about followed and follower users that I will ever need. Pretty neat.
104	
105	## Database Model Representation
106	
107	Let's add followers to the database first. Here is the `followers` association table. Make sure you add it above the `User` model in *models.py*, so that later the model can reference it.
108	
109	*app/models.py*: Followers association table
110	
111	```
112	followers = sa.Table(
113	    'followers',
114	    db.metadata,
115	    sa.Column('follower_id', sa.Integer, sa.ForeignKey('user.id'),
116	              primary_key=True),
117	    sa.Column('followed_id', sa.Integer, sa.ForeignKey('user.id'),
118	              primary_key=True)
119	)
120	
121	```
122	
123	This is a direct translation of the association table from my diagram above. Note that I am not declaring this table as a model, like I did for the users and posts tables. Since this is an auxiliary table that has no data other than the foreign keys, I created it without an associated model class.
124	
125	The `sa.Table` class from SQLAlchemy directly represents a database table. The table name is given as first argument. The second argument is that *metadata*, the place where SQLAlchemy stores the information about all the tables in the database. When using Flask-SQLAlchemy, the metadata instance can be obtained with `db.metadata`. The columns of this table are instances of `sa.Column` initialized with the column name, type and options. For this table neither of the foreign keys will have unique values that can be used as a primary key on their own, but the pair of foreign keys combined is going to be unique. For that reason both columns are marked as primary keys. This is called a *compound primary key*.
126	
127	Now I can define two many-to-many relationship attributes in the users table:
128	
129	*app/models.py*: Many-to-many followers relationship
130	
131	```
132	class User(UserMixin, db.Model):
133	    # ...
134	    following: so.WriteOnlyMapped'User' = so.relationship(
135	        secondary=followers, primaryjoin=(followers.c.follower_id == id),
136	        secondaryjoin=(followers.c.followed_id == id),
137	        back_populates='followers')
138	    followers: so.WriteOnlyMapped'User' = so.relationship(
139	        secondary=followers, primaryjoin=(followers.c.followed_id == id),
140	        secondaryjoin=(followers.c.follower_id == id),
141	        back_populates='following')
142	
143	```
144	
145	The setup of this relationship is non-trivial. Like I did for the `posts` one-to-many relationship, I'm using the `so.relationship` function to define the relationship in the model class. But because this relationship uses the same model on both sides, both relationship attributes are defined together.
146	
147	This relationship links `User` instances to other `User` instances, so as a convention let's say that for a pair of users linked by this relationship, the left side user is following the right side user. I'm defining the relationship as seen from the left side user with the name `following`, because when I query this relationship from the left side I will get the list of users the left-side user is following. Conversely, the `followers` relationship starts from the right side and finds all the users that follow a given user.
148	
149	Both relationships are defined with the `so.WriteOnlyMapped` type, in the same way as the `posts` relationship. Let's examine the arguments to the `so.relationship()` calls one by one:
150	
151	- `secondary` configures the association table that is used for this relationship, which I defined right above this class.
152	
153	- `primaryjoin` indicates the condition that links the entity to the association table. In the `following` relationship, the user has to match the `follower_id` attribute of the association table, so the condition reflects that. The `followers.c.follower_id` expression references the `follower_id` column of the association table. In the `followers` relationship, the roles are reversed, so the user must match the `followed_id` column.
154	
155	- `secondaryjoin` indicates the condition that links the association table to the user on the other side of the relationship. In the `following` relationship, the user has to match the `followed_id` column, and in the `followers` relationship, the user has to match the `follower_id` column.
156	
157	Don't worry if this is hard to understand. I will show you how to work with these queries in a moment, and then everything will become clearer.
158	
159	The changes to the database need to be recorded in a new database migration:
160	
161	```
162	(venv) $ flask db migrate -m "followers"
163	INFO  alembic.runtime.migration Context impl SQLiteImpl.
164	INFO  alembic.runtime.migration Will assume non-transactional DDL.
165	INFO  alembic.autogenerate.compare Detected added table 'followers'
166	  Generating /home/miguel/microblog/migrations/versions/ae346256b650_followers.py ... done
167	
168	(venv) $ flask db upgrade
169	INFO  alembic.runtime.migration Context impl SQLiteImpl.
170	INFO  alembic.runtime.migration Will assume non-transactional DDL.
171	INFO  alembic.runtime.migration Running upgrade 37f06a334dbf -> ae346256b650, followers
172	
173	```
174	
175	## Adding and Removing "follows"
176	
177	Thanks to the SQLAlchemy ORM, a user following another user can be recorded in the database working with the `following` and `followers` relationships as if they were lists. For example, if I had two users stored in `user1` and `user2` variables, I can make the first follow the second with this simple statement:
178	
179	```
180	user1.following.add(user2)
181	
182	```
183	
184	To stop following the user, then I could do:
185	
186	```
187	user1.following.remove(user2)
188	
189	```
190	
191	Even though adding and removing followers is fairly easy, I want to promote reusability in my code, so I'm not going to sprinkle "adds" and "removes" through the code. Instead, I'm going to implement the "follow" and "unfollow" functionality as methods in the `User` model. It is always best to move the application logic away from view functions and into models or other auxiliary classes or modules, because as you will see later in this chapter, that makes unit testing much easier.
192	
193	Below are the changes in the user model to work with following relationships:
194	
195	*app/models.py*: Add and remove followers
196	
197	```
198	class User(UserMixin, db.Model):
199	    #...
200	
201	    def follow(self, user):
202	        if not self.is_following(user):
203	            self.following.add(user)
204	
205	    def unfollow(self, user):
206	        if self.is_following(user):
207	            self.following.remove(user)
208	
209	    def is_following(self, user):
210	        query = self.following.select().where(User.id == user.id)
211	        return db.session.scalar(query) is not None
212	
213	    def followers_count(self):
214	        query = sa.select(sa.func.count()).select_from(
215	            self.followers.select().subquery())
216	        return db.session.scalar(query)
217	
218	    def following_count(self):
219	        query = sa.select(sa.func.count()).select_from(
220	            self.following.select().subquery())
221	        return db.session.scalar(query)
222	
223	```
224	
225	The `follow()` and `unfollow()` methods use the `add()` and `remove()` methods of the write-only relationship object as I have shown above, but before they touch the relationship they use the `is_following()` supporting method to make sure the requested action makes sense. For example, if I ask `user1` to follow `user2`, but it turns out that this following relationship already exists in the database, I do not want to add a duplicate. The same logic can be applied to unfollowing.
226	
227	The `is_following()` method performs a query on the `following` relationship to see if a given user is already included in it. All write-only relationships have a `select()` method that constructs a query that returns all the elements in the relationship. In this case I do not need to request all the elements, I'm just looking for a specific user, so I can restrict the query with a `where()` clause.
228	
229	The `followers_count()` and `following_count()` methods return the follower and following counts for the user. This requires a different type of query, in which the results are not returned, but just their count is. The `sa.select()` clause for these queries specify the `sa.func.count()` function from SQLAlchemy, to indicate that I want to get the result of a function. The `select_from()` clause is then added with the query that needs to be counted. Whenever a query is included as part of a larger query, SQLAlchemy requires the inner query to be converted to a sub-query by calling the `subquery()` method.
230	
231	## Obtaining the Posts from Followed Users
232	
233	Support for followers in the database is almost complete, but I'm actually missing one important feature. In the index page of the application I'm going to show blog posts written by all the people that the logged-in user is following, so I need to come up with a database query that returns these posts.
234	
235	The most obvious solution is to use a query that returns the list of followed users, which would be `user.following.select()`. After executing this query, I can run a query to get the posts of each of the returned users. Once I have all the posts I can merge them into a single list and sort them by date. Sounds good? Well, not really.
236	
237	This approach has a couple of problems. What happens if a user is following a thousand people? I would need to execute a thousand database queries just to collect all the posts. And then I will need to merge and sort the thousand lists in memory. As a secondary problem, consider that the application's home page will eventually have *pagination* implemented, so it will not display all the available posts but just the first few, with a link to get more if desired. If I'm going to display posts sorted by their date, how can I know which posts are the most recent of all followed users combined, unless I get all the posts and sort them first? This is actually an awful solution that does not scale well.
238	
239	There is really no way to avoid this merging and sorting of blog posts, but doing it in the application results in a very inefficient process. This kind of work is what relational databases excel at. The database has indexes that allow it to perform the queries and the sorting in a much more efficient way that I can possibly do from my side. So what I really want is to come up with a single database query that defines the information that I want to get, and then let the database figure out how to extract that information in the most efficient way.
240	
241	Below you can see this query:
242	
243	*app/models.py*: Following posts query
244	
245	```
246	class User(UserMixin, db.Model):
247	    #...
248	    def following_posts(self):
249	        Author = so.aliased(User)
250	        Follower = so.aliased(User)
251	        return (
252	            sa.select(Post)
253	            .join(Post.author.of_type(Author))
254	            .join(Author.followers.of_type(Follower))
255	            .where(Follower.id == self.id)
256	            .order_by(Post.timestamp.desc())
257	        )
258	
259	```
260	
261	This is by far the most complex query I have used on this application. I'm going to try to decipher this query one piece at a time. Ignoring for now the two `so.aliased()` calls, when you look at the structure of this query you are going to notice that there are four main sections defined by two `join()` clauses, `where()` and `order_by()`:
262	
263	```
264	sa.select(Post)
265	    .join(...)
266	    .join(...)
267	    .where(...)
268	    .order_by(...)
269	
270	```
271	
272	### Joins
273	
274	To understand what a join operation does, let's look at an example. Let's assume that I have a `User` table with the following contents:
275	
276	id
277	username
278	
279	1
280	john
281	
282	2
283	susan
284	
285	3
286	mary
287	
288	4
289	david
290	
291	To keep things simple I am not showing all the fields in the user model, just the ones that are important for this query.
292	
293	Let's say that the `followers` association table says that user `john` is following users `susan` and `david`, user `susan` is following `mary` and user `mary` is following `david`. The data that represents the above is this:
294	
295	follower_id
296	followed_id
297	
298	1
299	2
300	
301	1
302	4
303	
304	2
305	3
306	
307	3
308	4
309	
310	Finally, the `posts` table contains one post from each user:
311	
312	id
313	text
314	user_id
315	
316	1
317	post from susan
318	2
319	
320	2
321	post from mary
322	3
323	
324	3
325	post from david
326	4
327	
328	4
329	post from john
330	1
331	
332	This table also omits some fields that are not part of this discussion.
333	
334	Here is the first part of the query, including the first `join()` clause, and for now eliminating the `of_type(Author)`, which I'll explain later:
335	
336	```
337	sa.select(Post)
338	    .join(Post.author)
339	
340	```
341	
342	The `select()` portion of the query defines the entity that needs to be obtained, which in this case is posts. What I'm doing next is *joining* the entries in the posts table with the `Post.author` relationship.
343	
344	A join is a database operation that combines rows from two tables, according to a given criteria. The combined table is a temporary table that does not physically exists in the database, but can be used during the query. When the `join()` clause is given a relationship as an argument, SQLAlchemy combines the rows from the left and right sides of the relationship.
345	
346	With the example data that I defined above, the result of the join operation on the `Post.author` relationship:
347	
348	post.id
349	post.text
350	post.user_id
351	user.id
352	user.username
353	
354	1
355	post from susan
356	2
357	2
358	susan
359	
360	2
361	post from mary
362	3
363	3
364	mary
365	
366	3
367	post from david
368	4
369	4
370	david
371	
372	4
373	post from john
374	1
375	1
376	john
377	
378	You may notice that the `post.user_id` and `user.id` columns in the joined table have always the same value. Because I asked for a join on the `Post.author` relationship, which links posts to their authors, SQLAlchemy knows that it needs to match rows from the posts table with rows from the users table.
379	
380	Effectively, what the join above does is create an extended table that provides access to posts, along with information about the author of each post.
381	
382	Unfortunately things have to get more complicated now, because next I need to join the combined table above again to add the followers. Once I have a combined table with each row having a follower with a post they're following and the author of such post, then I can easily filter to find the posts that any given user should see.
383	
384	One reason why this is so complicated is that for this query we need to treat users in two capacities. In the join above users are authors of posts, but in the second join I need to consider users as followers of other users. To be able to clearly tell SQLAlchemy how to join all these tables, I need to have a way to refer to users independently as authors and as followers. The `so.aliased()` calls are used to create two references to the `User` model that I can use in the query. 
385	
386	So the first join in this query, which is concerned with combining posts with their authors, can be written as follows:
387	
388	```
389	Author = so.aliased(User)
390	sa.select(Post)
391	    .join(Post.author.of_type(Author))
392	
393	```
394	
395	Here the `of_type(Author)` qualifier on the joined relationship tells SQLAlchemy that in the rest of the query I'm going to refer to the right side entity of the relationship with the `Author` alias.
396	
397	Let's now look at the second join in the query:
398	
399	```
400	Author = so.aliased(User)
401	Follower = so.aliased(User)
402	sa.select(Post)
403	    .join(Post.author.of_type(Author))
404	    .join(Author.followers.of_type(Follower))
405	
406	```
407	
408	For the second join I want SQLAlchemy to join on the `Author.followers` relationship, with `Author` being the alias for `User` defined above. This is a many-to-many relationship, so the `followers` association table must implicitly be part of the join as well. The users that are added to the combined table as a result of this new join will use the `Follower` alias.
409	
410	The `User.followers` relationship has followed users on the left side, defined by the `followed_id` foreign in the association table, and their followers on the right side, defined by the `follower_id` foreign key. Using the example `followers` association table above, the table that combines the posts, with their authors, and their followers is:
411	
412	post.id
413	post.text
414	post.user_id
415	author.id
416	author.username
417	follower.id
418	follower.username
419	
420	1
421	post from susan
422	2
423	2
424	susan
425	1
426	john
427	
428	2
429	post from mary
430	3
431	3
432	mary
433	2
434	susan
435	
436	3
437	post from david
438	4
439	4
440	david
441	1
442	john
443	
444	3
445	post from david
446	4
447	4
448	david
449	3
450	mary
451	
452	There are a couple of interesting things to mention about the results of this join. First of all, now each row in the table has users that are authors and users that are followers, so it is necessary to use the aliases to avoid confusion.
453	
454	The post with `post.id == 3` appears twice in this joined table. Can you tell why? The author of this post is `david`, with `user.id == 4`. Looking for this user in the `followers` association table under the `followed_id` foreign key there are two entries for users 1 and 3, which means that `david` is followed by `john` and `mary`. Since both users have to be joined to this post written by `david`, the join operation creates two rows with this post, each with one of the joined users.
455	
456	There is also one post that does not appear at all. This is `post.id == 4`, written by `john`. According to the `followers` association table nobody is following this user, so there are no followers that can be matched with it, and for that reason the join drops this post from the results.
457	
458	### Filters
459	
460	The join operation gave me a list of all the posts that are followed by a user, which is a lot more data that I really want. I'm only interested in a subset of this list, the posts followed by only one user, so I need to remove all the entries I don't need, which I can do with a `where()` clause.
461	
462	Here is the filter portion of the query:
463	
464	```
465	    .where(Follower.id == self.id)
466	
467	```
468	
469	Since this query is in a method of class `User`, the `self.id` expression refers to the I'm interested in retrieving posts for. The `where()` call selects the items in the joined table that have this user as a follower. Remember that `Follower` in this query is an alias to `User` that is necessary so that SQLAlchemy knows which one of the two users that are included in each row in the table the filter is based on.
470	
471	Let's say the user I'm interested in is `john`, which has its `id` field set to 1. Here is how the joined table looks after the filtering:
472	
473	post.id
474	post.text
475	post.user_id
476	author.id
477	author.username
478	follower.id
479	follower.username
480	
481	1
482	post from susan
483	2
484	2
485	susan
486	1
487	john
488	
489	3
490	post from david
491	4
492	4
493	david
494	1
495	john
496	
497	And these are exactly the posts that I wanted!
498	
499	Remember that the query was issued on the `Post` class, so even though I ended up with a larger table that was created by the database as part of this query, the result will be the posts that are included in this temporary table, without the extra columns added by the join operation.
500	
501	### Sorting
502	
503	The final step of the process is to sort the results. The part of the query that does that says:
504	
505	```
506	    .order_by(Post.timestamp.desc())
507	
508	```
509	
510	Here I'm saying that I want the results sorted by the timestamp field of the post in descending order. With this ordering, the first result will be the most recent blog post.
511	
512	## Combining Own and Followed Posts
513	
514	The query that I'm using in the `following_posts()` function is extremely useful, but has one limitation. People expect to see their own posts included in their timeline of followed users, and the query as defined above does not include the user's own posts.
515	
516	There are two possible ways to expand this query to include the user's own posts. The most straightforward way is to leave the query as it is, but make sure all users are following themselves. If you are your own follower, then the query as shown above will find your own posts along with those of all the people you follow. The disadvantage of this method is that it affects the counts of followers. All follower counts are going to be inflated by one, so they'll have to be adjusted before they are shown.
517	
518	The other way to do this is make the expand the query logic so that the results come either from the followed posts or from the user's own.
519	
520	After considering both options I decided to go with the second one. Below you can see the `following_posts()` method after it has been expanded to include the user's posts through a union:
521	
522	*app/models.py*: Followed posts query with user's own posts.
523	
524	```
525	    def following_posts(self):
526	        Author = so.aliased(User)
527	        Follower = so.aliased(User)
528	        return (
529	            sa.select(Post)
530	            .join(Post.author.of_type(Author))
531	            .join(Author.followers.of_type(Follower), isouter=True)
532	            .where(sa.or_(
533	                Follower.id == self.id,
534	                Author.id == self.id,
535	            ))
536	            .group_by(Post)
537	            .order_by(Post.timestamp.desc())
538	        )
539	
540	```
541	
542	The structure of this query is now as follows:
543	
544	```
545	sa.select(Post)
546	    .join(...)
547	    .join(..., isouter=True)
548	    .where(sa.or_(..., ...))
549	    .group_by(...)
550	    .order_by(...)
551	
552	```
553	
554	### Outer Joins
555	
556	The second join is now an *outer join*. Do you remember what happened to the post written by `john` in the previous section? When the second join was calculated, this post was dropped, because this user had no followers. To be able to include the user's own posts, first it is necessary to change the join to keep posts that have no matches on the right side of the join. The joins used in the previous section are called *inner joins*, and only preserve entries from the left side that have a matching entry on the right. The `isouter=True` option tells SQLAlchemy to use a *left outer join* instead, which preserves items from the left side that have no match on the right.
557	
558	When using a left outer join, the joined table is:
559	
560	post.id
561	post.text
562	post.user_id
563	author.id
564	author.username
565	follower.id
566	follower.username
567	
568	1
569	post from susan
570	2
571	2
572	susan
573	1
574	john
575	
576	2
577	post from mary
578	3
579	3
580	mary
581	2
582	susan
583	
584	3
585	post from david
586	4
587	4
588	david
589	1
590	john
591	
592	3
593	post from david
594	4
595	4
596	david
597	3
598	mary
599	
600	4
601	post from john
602	1
603	1
604	john
605	null
606	null
607	
608	The outer join ensures that the joined table has at least one occurrence of every post that is in the posts table.
609	
610	### Compound Filters
611	
612	The joined table now has all the posts, so I can expand the `where()` clause to include both posts from followed users as well as own posts. SQLAlchemy provides the `sa.or_()`, `sa.and_()` and `sa.not_()` helpers to create compound conditions. In this case I need to use `sa.or_()` to specify that I have two options for selecting posts.
613	
614	Let's review the updated filter:
615	
616	```
617	    .where(sa.or_(
618	        Follower.id == self.id,
619	        Author.id == self.id,
620	    ))
621	
622	```
623	
624	Here once again I take advantage of the aliases, without which it would be impossible to describe what I want to do. What I'm saying with this condition is that I want to get posts that have the user as a follower, or posts that have the user as the author.
625	
626	Using `john` once again as an example, the filtered table would be:
627	
628	post.id
629	post.text
630	post.user_id
631	author.id
632	author.username
633	follower.id
634	follower.username
635	
636	1
637	post from susan
638	2
639	2
640	susan
641	1
642	john
643	
644	3
645	post from david
646	4
647	4
648	david
649	1
650	john
651	
652	4
653	post from john
654	1
655	1
656	john
657	null
658	null
659	
660	And this is perfect, as this list contains the two followed posts plus the user's own.
661	
662	### Grouping
663	
664	Instead of using `john`, let's try filtering for `david`:
665	
666	post.id
667	post.text
668	post.user_id
669	author.id
670	author.username
671	follower.id
672	follower.username
673	
674	3
675	post from david
676	4
677	4
678	david
679	1
680	john
681	
682	3
683	post from david
684	4
685	4
686	david
687	3
688	mary
689	
690	This user isn't following anyone, so the only results come from posts that the user authored. But there is a complication, in that this user wrote a single post, but due to the requirements of the joins, that post had to be duplicated, and the filtering does nothing to eliminate duplicates.
691	
692	Duplication on joined tables is actually very common. If you look at the complete joined table you can also see that `john` appears twice as a follower. Because these joins include a many-to-many relationship, both sides can end up with duplicates as the database matches entities on both sides.
693	
694	To eliminate the duplicates in the final list of results, there is a `group_by()` clause that can be added to the query. This clause looks at the results after filtering has been done, and eliminates any duplicates of the provided arguments. For this query I want to ensure that there are no duplicate posts, so I pass `Post` as an argument, which SQLAlchemy will interpret as all the attributes of the model.
695	
696	## Unit Testing the User Model
697	
698	While I don't consider the followers implementation I have built a "complex" feature, I think it is not trivial. My concern when I write non-trivial code, is to ensure that this code will continue to work in the future, as I make modifications on different parts of the application. The best way to ensure that code you have already written continues to work in the future is to create a suite of automated tests that you can re-run each time changes are made.
699	
700	Python includes a very useful `unittest` package that makes it easy to write and execute unit tests. Let's write some unit tests for the existing methods in the `User` class in a *tests.py* module:
701	
702	*tests.py*: User model unit tests.
703	
704	```
705	import os
706	os.environ'DATABASE_URL' = 'sqlite://'
707	
708	from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
709	import unittest
710	from app import app, db
711	from app.models import User, Post
712	
713	class UserModelCase(unittest.TestCase):
714	    def setUp(self):
715	        self.app_context = app.app_context()
716	        self.app_context.push()
717	        db.create_all()
718	
719	    def tearDown(self):
720	        db.session.remove()
721	        db.drop_all()
722	        self.app_context.pop()
723	
724	    def test_password_hashing(self):
725	        u = User(username='susan', email='susan@example.com')
726	        u.set_password('cat')
727	        self.assertFalse(u.check_password('dog'))
728	        self.assertTrue(u.check_password('cat'))
729	
730	    def test_avatar(self):
731	        u = User(username='john', email='john@example.com')
732	        self.assertEqual(u.avatar(128), ('https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/'
733	                                         'd4c74594d841139328695756648b6bd6'
734	                                         '?d=identicon&s=128'))
735	
736	    def test_follow(self):
737	        u1 = User(username='john', email='john@example.com')
738	        u2 = User(username='susan', email='susan@example.com')
739	        db.session.add(u1)
740	        db.session.add(u2)
741	        db.session.commit()
742	        following = db.session.scalars(u1.following.select()).all()
743	        followers = db.session.scalars(u2.followers.select()).all()
744	        self.assertEqual(following, )
745	        self.assertEqual(followers, )
746	
747	        u1.follow(u2)
748	        db.session.commit()
749	        self.assertTrue(u1.is_following(u2))
750	        self.assertEqual(u1.following_count(), 1)
751	        self.assertEqual(u2.followers_count(), 1)
752	        u1_following = db.session.scalars(u1.following.select()).all()
753	        u2_followers = db.session.scalars(u2.followers.select()).all()
754	        self.assertEqual(u1_following0.username, 'susan')
755	        self.assertEqual(u2_followers0.username, 'john')
756	
757	        u1.unfollow(u2)
758	        db.session.commit()
759	        self.assertFalse(u1.is_following(u2))
760	        self.assertEqual(u1.following_count(), 0)
761	        self.assertEqual(u2.followers_count(), 0)
762	
763	    def test_follow_posts(self):
764	        # create four users
765	        u1 = User(username='john', email='john@example.com')
766	        u2 = User(username='susan', email='susan@example.com')
767	        u3 = User(username='mary', email='mary@example.com')
768	        u4 = User(username='david', email='david@example.com')
769	        db.session.add_all(u1, u2, u3, u4)
770	
771	        # create four posts
772	        now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
773	        p1 = Post(body="post from john", author=u1,
774	                  timestamp=now + timedelta(seconds=1))
775	        p2 = Post(body="post from susan", author=u2,
776	                  timestamp=now + timedelta(seconds=4))
777	        p3 = Post(body="post from mary", author=u3,
778	                  timestamp=now + timedelta(seconds=3))
779	        p4 = Post(body="post from david", author=u4,
780	                  timestamp=now + timedelta(seconds=2))
781	        db.session.add_all(p1, p2, p3, p4)
782	        db.session.commit()
783	
784	        # setup the followers
785	        u1.follow(u2)  # john follows susan
786	        u1.follow(u4)  # john follows david
787	        u2.follow(u3)  # susan follows mary
788	        u3.follow(u4)  # mary follows david
789	        db.session.commit()
790	
791	        # check the following posts of each user
792	        f1 = db.session.scalars(u1.following_posts()).all()
793	        f2 = db.session.scalars(u2.following_posts()).all()
794	        f3 = db.session.scalars(u3.following_posts()).all()
795	        f4 = db.session.scalars(u4.following_posts()).all()
796	        self.assertEqual(f1, p2, p4, p1)
797	        self.assertEqual(f2, p2, p3)
798	        self.assertEqual(f3, p3, p4)
799	        self.assertEqual(f4, p4)
800	
801	if __name__ == '__main__':
802	    unittest.main(verbosity=2)
803	
804	```
805	
806	I have added four tests that exercise the password hashing, user avatar and followers functionality in the user model. The `setUp()` and `tearDown()` methods are special methods that the unit testing framework executes before and after each test respectively.
807	
808	I have implemented a little hack to prevent the unit tests from using the regular database that I use for development. By setting the `DATABASE_URL` environment variable to `sqlite://`, I change the application configuration to direct SQLAlchemy to use an in-memory SQLite database during the tests. This is important, as I don't want the tests to make changes to the database that I'm using myself.
809	
810	The `setUp()` method then creates an *application context* and pushes it. This ensures that the Flask application instance, along with its configuration data is accessible to Flask extensions. Don't worry if this does not make a lot of sense at this point, as this will be covered in more detail later.
811	
812	The `db.create_all()` call creates all the database tables. This is a quick way to create a database from scratch that is useful for testing. For development and production use I have already shown you how to create database tables through database migrations.
813	
814	You can run the entire test suite with the following command:
815	
816	```
817	(venv) $ python tests.py
818	2023-11-19 14:51:07,578 INFO in __init__: Microblog startup
819	test_avatar (__main__.UserModelCase.test_avatar) ... ok
820	test_follow (__main__.UserModelCase.test_follow) ... ok
821	test_follow_posts (__main__.UserModelCase.test_follow_posts) ... ok
822	test_password_hashing (__main__.UserModelCase.test_password_hashing) ... ok
823	
824	----------------------------------------------------------------------
825	Ran 4 tests in 0.259s
826	
827	OK
828	
829	```
830	
831	From now on, every time a change is made to the application, you can re-run the tests to make sure the features that are being tested have not been affected. Also, each time another feature is added to the application, a unit test should be written for it.
832	
833	## Integrating Followers with the Application
834	
835	The support of followers in the database and models is now complete, but I don't have any of this functionality incorporated into the application, so I'm going to add that now.
836	
837	Because the follow and unfollow actions introduce changes in the application, I'm going to implement them as `POST` requests, which are triggered from the web browser as a result of submitting a web form. It would be easier to implement these routes as `GET` requests, but then they could be exploited in CSRF attacks. Because `GET` requests are harder to protect against CSRF, they should only be used on actions that do not introduce state changes. Implementing these as a result of a form submission is better because then a CSRF token can be added to the form.
838	
839	But how can a follow or unfollow action be triggered from a web form when the only thing the user needs to do is click on "Follow" or "Unfollow", without submitting any data? To make this work, the form is going to be empty. The only elements in the form are going to be the CSRF token, which is implemented as a hidden field and added automatically by Flask-WTF, and a submit button, which is going to be what the user needs to click to trigger the action. Since the two actions are almost identical I'm going to use the same form for both. I'm going to call this form `EmptyForm`.
840	
841	*app/forms.py*: Empty form for following and unfollowing.
842	
843	```
844	class EmptyForm(FlaskForm):
845	    submit = SubmitField('Submit')
846	
847	```
848	
849	Let's add two new routes in the application to follow and unfollow a user:
850	
851	*app/routes.py*: Follow and unfollow routes.
852	
853	```
854	from app.forms import EmptyForm
855	
856	# ...
857	
858	@app.route('/follow/<username>', methods='POST')
859	@login_required
860	def follow(username):
861	    form = EmptyForm()
862	    if form.validate_on_submit():
863	        user = db.session.scalar(
864	            sa.select(User).where(User.username == username))
865	        if user is None:
866	            flash(f'User {username} not found.')
867	            return redirect(url_for('index'))
868	        if user == current_user:
869	            flash('You cannot follow yourself!')
870	            return redirect(url_for('user', username=username))
871	        current_user.follow(user)
872	        db.session.commit()
873	        flash(f'You are following {username}!')
874	        return redirect(url_for('user', username=username))
875	    else:
876	        return redirect(url_for('index'))
877	
878	@app.route('/unfollow/<username>', methods='POST')
879	@login_required
880	def unfollow(username):
881	    form = EmptyForm()
882	    if form.validate_on_submit():
883	        user = db.session.scalar(
884	            sa.select(User).where(User.username == username))
885	        if user is None:
886	            flash(f'User {username} not found.')
887	            return redirect(url_for('index'))
888	        if user == current_user:
889	            flash('You cannot unfollow yourself!')
890	            return redirect(url_for('user', username=username))
891	        current_user.unfollow(user)
892	        db.session.commit()
893	        flash(f'You are not following {username}.')
894	        return redirect(url_for('user', username=username))
895	    else:
896	        return redirect(url_for('index'))
897	
898	```
899	
900	The form handling in these routes is simpler, because we only have to implement the submission part. Unlike other forms such as the login and edit profile forms, these two forms do not have their own pages, the forms will be rendered by the `user()` route and will appear in the user's profile page. The only reason why the `validate_on_submit()` call can fail is if the CSRF token is missing or invalid, so in that case I just redirect the application back to the home page.
901	
902	If the form validation passes, I do some error checking before actually carrying out the follow or unfollow action. This is to prevent unexpected issues, and to try to provide a useful message to the user when a problem has occurred.
903	
904	To render the follow or unfollow button, I need to instantiate an `EmptyForm` object and pass it to the *user.html* template. Because these two actions are mutually exclusive, I can pass a single instance of this generic form to the template:
905	
906	*app/routes.py*: Follow and unfollow routes.
907	
908	```
909	@app.route('/user/<username>')
910	@login_required
911	def user(username):
912	    # ...
913	    form = EmptyForm()
914	    return render_template('user.html', user=user, posts=posts, form=form)
915	
916	```
917	
918	I can now add the follow or unfollow forms in the profile page of each user:
919	
920	*app/templates/user.html*: Follow and unfollow links in user profile page.
921	
922	```
923	        ...
924	        <h1>User: {{ user.username }}</h1>
925	        {% if user.about_me %}<p>{{ user.about_me }}</p>{% endif %}
926	        {% if user.last_seen %}<p>Last seen on: {{ user.last_seen }}</p>{% endif %}
927	        <p>{{ user.followers_count() }} followers, {{ user.following_count() }} following.</p>
928	        {% if user == current_user %}
929	        <p><a href="{{ url_for('edit_profile') }}">Edit your profile</a></p>
930	        {% elif not current_user.is_following(user) %}
931	        <p>
932	            <form action="{{ url_for('follow', username=user.username) }}" method="post">
933	                {{ form.hidden_tag() }}
934	                {{ form.submit(value='Follow') }}
935	            </form>
936	        </p>
937	        {% else %}
938	        <p>
939	            <form action="{{ url_for('unfollow', username=user.username) }}" method="post">
940	                {{ form.hidden_tag() }}
941	                {{ form.submit(value='Unfollow') }}
942	            </form>
943	        </p>
944	        {% endif %}
945	        ...
946	
947	```
948	
949	The changes to the user profile template add a line below the last seen timestamp that shows how many followers and following users this user has. And the line that has the "Edit" link when you are viewing your own profile now can have one of three possible links:
950	
951	- If the user is viewing their own profile, the "Edit" link shows as before.
952	
953	- If the user is viewing a user that is not currently followed, the "Follow" form shows.
954	
955	- If the user is viewing a user that is currently followed, the "Unfollow" form shows.
956	
957	To reuse the `EmptyForm()` instance for both the follow and unfollow forms, I pass a `value` argument when rendering the submit button. In a submit button, the `value` attribute defines the label, so with this trick I can change the text in the submit button depending on the action that I need to present to the user.
958	
959	At this point you can run the application, create a few users and play with following and unfollowing users. The only thing you need to remember is to type the profile page URL of the user you want to follow or unfollow, since there is currently no way to see a list of users. For example, if you want to follow a user with the `susan` username, you will need to type *http://localhost:5000/user/susan* in the browser's address bar to access the profile page for that user. Make sure you check how the followed and follower user counts change as you issue follows or unfollows.
960	
961	I should be showing the list of followed posts in the index page of the application, but I don't have all the pieces in place to do that yet, since users cannot write blog posts in the current version of the application. So I'm going to delay this change until that functionality is in place.
962	
963	Continue on to the next chapter.
964	
965	##
966	
967	---
968	> [[Flask Mega-Tutorial 学习笔记|返回目录]]
969

1 # Part 9: Pagination 2 3 > 来源: [https://blog.miguelgrinberg.com/post/the-flask-mega-tutorial-part-ix-pagination](https://blog.miguelgrinberg.com/post/the-flask-mega-tutorial-part-ix-pagination) | Flask Mega-Tutorial by Miguel Grinberg 4 5 --- 6 7 This is the ninth installment of the Flask Mega-Tutorial series, in which I'm going to tell you how to paginate lists of database entries. 8 9 You are reading the 2024 edition of the Flask Mega-Tutorial. The complete course is also available to order in e-book and paperback formats from Amazon. Thank you for your support! 10 11 If you are looking for the 2018 edition of this course, you can find it here. 12 13 For your reference, here is the complete list of articles in this series: 14 15 - Chapter 1: Hello, World! 16 17 - Chapter 2: Templates 18 19 - Chapter 3: Web Forms 20 21 - Chapter 4: Database 22 23 - Chapter 5: User Logins 24 25 - Chapter 6: Profile Page and Avatars 26 27 - Chapter 7: Error Handling 28 29 - Chapter 8: Followers 30 31 - Chapter 9: Pagination (this article) 32 33 - Chapter 10: Email Support 34 35 - Chapter 11: Facelift 36 37 - Chapter 12: Dates and Times 38 39 - Chapter 13: I18n and L10n 40 41 - Chapter 14: Ajax 42 43 - Chapter 15: A Better Application Structure 44 45 - Chapter 16: Full-Text Search 46 47 - Chapter 17: Deployment on Linux 48 49 - Chapter 18: Deployment on Heroku 50 51 - Chapter 19: Deployment on Docker Containers 52 53 - Chapter 20: Some JavaScript Magic 54 55 - Chapter 21: User Notifications 56 57 - Chapter 22: Background Jobs 58 59 - Chapter 23: Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) 60 61 In Chapter 8 I have made several database changes that were necessary to support the "follower" paradigm that is so popular with social networks. With that functionality in place, I'm ready to remove the last piece of scaffolding that I have put in place in the beginning, the fake posts. In this chapter the application will start accepting blog posts from users, and also deliver them in the home and profile pages as a paginated list. 62 63 *The GitHub links for this chapter are: Browse, Zip, Diff.* 64 65 ## Submission of Blog Posts 66 67 The home page needs to have a form in which users can type new posts. First I create a form class: 68 69 *app/forms.py*: Blog submission form. 70 71 ``` 72 class PostForm(FlaskForm): 73 post = TextAreaField('Say something', validators= 74 DataRequired(), Length(min=1, max=140)) 75 submit = SubmitField('Submit') 76 77 ``` 78 79 Next, I can add this form to the template for the main page of the application: 80 81 *app/templates/index.html*: Post submission form in index template 82 83 ``` 84 {% extends "base.html" %} 85 86 {% block content %} 87

Hi, {{ current_user.username }}!

88
89 {{ form.hidden_tag() }} 90

91 {{ form.post.label }}
92 {{ form.post(cols=32, rows=4) }}
93 {% for error in form.post.errors %} 94 {{ error }} 95 {% endfor %} 96

97

{{ form.submit() }}

98
99 {% for post in posts %} 100

101 {{ post.author.username }} says: {{ post.body }} 102

103 {% endfor %} 104 {% endblock %} 105 106 ``` 107 108 The changes in this template are similar to how previous forms were handled. The final part is to add the form creation and handling in the view function: 109 110 *app/routes.py*: Post submission form in index view function. 111 112 ``` 113 from app.forms import PostForm 114 from app.models import Post 115 116 @app.route('/', methods='GET', 'POST') 117 @app.route('/index', methods='GET', 'POST') 118 @login_required 119 def index(): 120 form = PostForm() 121 if form.validate_on_submit(): 122 post = Post(body=form.post.data, author=current_user) 123 db.session.add(post) 124 db.session.commit() 125 flash('Your post is now live!') 126 return redirect(url_for('index')) 127 posts = 128 { 129 'author': {'username': 'John'}, 130 'body': 'Beautiful day in Portland!' 131 }, 132 { 133 'author': {'username': 'Susan'}, 134 'body': 'The Avengers movie was so cool!' 135 } 136 137 return render_template("index.html", title='Home Page', form=form, 138 posts=posts) 139 140 ``` 141 142 Let's review the changes in this view function one by one: 143 144 - I'm now importing the `Post` and `PostForm` classes 145 146 - I accept `POST` requests in both routes associated with the `index` view function in addition to `GET` requests, since this view function will now receive form data. 147 148 - The form processing logic inserts a new `Post` record into the database. 149 150 - The template receives the `form` object as an additional argument, so that it can render it to the page. 151 152 Before I continue, I wanted to mention something important related to processing of web forms. Notice how after I process the form data, I end the request by issuing a redirect to the home page. I could have easily skipped the redirect and allowed the function to continue down into the template rendering part, since this is already the index view function. 153 154 So, why issue a redirect to the same page? Because it is a standard practice to always respond to a `POST` request generated by a web form submission with a redirect. This helps mitigate an annoyance with how the refresh command is implemented in web browsers. When you hit the refresh key, the web browser just re-issues the last request. If the last request is a `POST` request with a form submission, then a refresh will re-submit the form. Because this is unexpected, the browser is going to ask the user to confirm the duplicate submission, but most users will not understand what the browser is asking them. If a `POST` request is answered with a redirect, the browser is instructed to send a `GET` request to grab the page indicated in the redirect, so now the last request is not a `POST` request anymore, and the refresh command works in a more predictable way. 155 156 This simple trick is called the Post/Redirect/Get pattern. It avoids inserting duplicate posts when a user inadvertently refreshes the page after submitting a web form. 157 158 ## Displaying Blog Posts 159 160 If you recall, I created a couple of fake blog posts that I've been displaying in the home page for a long time. These fake objects are created explicitly in the `index` view function as a simple Python list: 161 162 ``` 163 posts = 164 { 165 'author': {'username': 'John'}, 166 'body': 'Beautiful day in Portland!' 167 }, 168 { 169 'author': {'username': 'Susan'}, 170 'body': 'The Avengers movie was so cool!' 171 } 172 173 174 ``` 175 176 But now I have the `following_posts()` method in the `User` model that returns a query for the posts that a given user wants to see. So now I can replace the fake posts with real posts: 177 178 *app/routes.py*: Display real posts in home page. 179 180 ``` 181 @app.route('/', methods='GET', 'POST') 182 @app.route('/index', methods='GET', 'POST') 183 @login_required 184 def index(): 185 # ... 186 posts = db.session.scalars(current_user.following_posts()).all() 187 return render_template("index.html", title='Home Page', form=form, 188 posts=posts) 189 190 ``` 191 192 The `following_posts` method of the `User` class returns a SQLAlchemy query object that is configured to grab the posts the user is interested in from the database. After executing this query and calling `all()` on the results object the `posts` variable ends up with a list with all the results. So I end up with a structure that is very much like the one with fake posts that I have been using until now. It's so close that the template does not even need to change. 193 194 ## Making It Easier to Find Users to Follow 195 196 As I'm sure you noticed, the application does not do a great job at letting users find other users to follow. In fact, there is actually no way to see what other users are there at all. I'm going to address that with a few simple changes. 197 198 I'm going to create a new page that I'm going to call the "Explore" page. This page will work like the home page, but instead of only showing posts from followed users, it will show a global post stream from all users. Here is the new explore view function: 199 200 *app/routes.py*: Explore view function. 201 202 ``` 203 @app.route('/explore') 204 @login_required 205 def explore(): 206 query = sa.select(Post).order_by(Post.timestamp.desc()) 207 posts = db.session.scalars(query).all() 208 return render_template('index.html', title='Explore', posts=posts) 209 210 ``` 211 212 Did you notice something odd in this view function? The `render_template()` call references the *index.html* template, which I'm using in the main page of the application. Since this page is going to be very similar to the main page, I decided to reuse the template. But one difference with the main page is that in the explore page I do not want to have a form to write blog posts, so in this view function I did not include the `form` argument in the `render_template()` call. 213 214 To prevent the *index.html* template from crashing when it tries to render a web form that does not exist, I'm going to add a conditional that only renders the form if it was passed by the view function: 215 216 *app/templates/index.html*: Make the blog post submission form optional. 217 218 ``` 219 {% extends "base.html" %} 220 221 {% block content %} 222

Hi, {{ current_user.username }}!

223 {% if form %} 224
225 ... 226
227 {% endif %} 228 ... 229 {% endblock %} 230 231 ``` 232 233 I'm also going to add a link to this new page in the navigation bar, right after the index page link: 234 235 *app/templates/base.html*: Link to explore page in navigation bar. 236 237 ``` 238 Explore 239 240 ``` 241 242 Remember the *_post.html* sub-template that I have introduced in Chapter 6 to render blog posts in the user profile page? This was a small template that was included from the user profile page template, and was written in a separate file so that it can also be used from other templates. I'm now going to make a small improvement to it, which is to show the username of the blog post author as a clickable link: 243 244 *app/templates/_post.html*: Show link to author in blog posts. 245 246 ``` 247 248 249 250 256 257
251 252 {{ post.author.username }} 253 254 says:
{{ post.body }} 255
258 259 ``` 260 261 I can now use this sub-template to render blog posts in the home and explore pages: 262 263 *app/templates/index.html*: Use blog post sub-template. 264 265 ``` 266 ... 267 {% for post in posts %} 268 {% include '_post.html' %} 269 {% endfor %} 270 ... 271 272 ``` 273 274 The sub-template expects a variable named `post` to exist, and that is how the loop variable in the index template is named, so that works perfectly. 275 276 With these small changes, the usability of the application has improved considerably. Now a user can visit the explore page to read blog posts from unknown users and based on those posts find new users to follow, which can be done by simply clicking on a username to access the profile page. Amazing, right? 277 278 At this point I suggest you try the application once again, so that you experience these last user interface improvements. You may also want to create a few different users and write posts as them, so that the system has a variety of content from multiple users. 279 280 ## Pagination of Blog Posts 281 282 The application is looking better than ever, but showing all the followed posts in the home page is going to become a problem sooner rather than later. What happens if a user has a thousand followed posts? Or a million? As you can imagine, managing such a large list of posts will be extremely slow and inefficient. 283 284 To address that problem, I'm going to *paginate* the post list. This means that initially I'm going to show just a limited number of posts at a time, and include links to navigate forward and backward through the complete list of posts. Flask-SQLAlchemy supports pagination natively with the `db.paginate()` function, which works like `db.session.scalars()`, but with pagination built-in. If for example, I want to get the first twenty followed posts of the user, I can do this: 285 286 ``` 287 >>> query = sa.select(Post).order_by(Post.timestamp.desc()) 288 >>> posts = db.paginate(query, page=1, per_page=20, error_out=False).items 289 290 ``` 291 292 The `paginate` function can be called on any query. It takes a few arguments, of which the following three are the most significant: 293 294 - `page`: the page number, starting from 1 295 296 - `per_page`: the number of items per page 297 298 - `error_out`: an error flag. If `True`, when an out of range page is requested a 404 error will be automatically returned to the client. If `False`, an empty list will be returned for out of range pages. 299 300 The return value from `db.paginate()` is a `Pagination` object. The `items` attribute of this object contains the list of items in the requested page. There are other useful things in the `Pagination` object that I will discuss later. 301 302 Now let's think about how I can implement pagination in the `index()` view function. I can start by adding a configuration item to the application that determines how many items will be displayed per page. 303 304 *config.py*: Posts per page configuration. 305 306 ``` 307 class Config: 308 # ... 309 POSTS_PER_PAGE = 3 310 311 ``` 312 313 It is a good idea to have these application-wide "knobs" that can change behaviors in the configuration file, because then I can go to a single place to make adjustments. In the final application I will of course use a larger number than three items per page, but for testing it is useful to work with small numbers. 314 315 Next, I need to decide how the page number is going to be incorporated into application URLs. A fairly common way is to use a *query string* argument to specify an optional page number, defaulting to page 1 if it is not given. Here are some example URLs that show how I'm going to implement this: 316 317 - Page 1, implicit: *http://localhost:5000/index* 318 319 - Page 1, explicit: *http://localhost:5000/index?page=1* 320 321 - Page 3: *http://localhost:5000/index?page=3* 322 323 To access arguments given in the query string, I can use the Flask's `request.args` object. You have seen this already in Chapter 5, when I implemented user login URLs from Flask-Login that can sometimes include a `next` query string argument. 324 325 Below you can see how I added pagination to the home and explore view functions: 326 327 *app/routes.py*: Followers association table 328 329 ``` 330 @app.route('/', methods='GET', 'POST') 331 @app.route('/index', methods='GET', 'POST') 332 @login_required 333 def index(): 334 # ... 335 page = request.args.get('page', 1, type=int) 336 posts = db.paginate(current_user.following_posts(), page=page, 337 per_page=app.config'POSTS_PER_PAGE', error_out=False) 338 return render_template('index.html', title='Home', form=form, 339 posts=posts.items) 340 341 @app.route('/explore') 342 @login_required 343 def explore(): 344 page = request.args.get('page', 1, type=int) 345 query = sa.select(Post).order_by(Post.timestamp.desc()) 346 posts = db.paginate(query, page=page, 347 per_page=app.config'POSTS_PER_PAGE', error_out=False) 348 return render_template("index.html", title='Explore', posts=posts.items) 349 350 ``` 351 352 With these changes, the two routes determine the page number to display, either from the `page` query string argument or a default of 1, and then use the `paginate()` method to retrieve only the desired page of results. The `POSTS_PER_PAGE` configuration item that determines the page size is accessed through the `app.config` object. 353 354 Note how short these changes are, and how little code is affected each time a change is made. I am trying to write each part of the application without making any assumptions about how the other parts work, and this enables me to write modular and robust applications that are easier to extend and to test, and are less likely to fail or have bugs. 355 356 Go ahead and try the pagination support. First make sure you have more than three blog posts written. This is easier to see in the explore page, which shows posts from all users. You are now going to see just the three most recent posts. If you want to see the next three, type *http://localhost:5000/explore?page=2* in your browser's address bar. 357 358 ## Page Navigation 359 360 The next change is to add links at the bottom of the blog post list that allow users to navigate to the next and/or previous pages. Remember that I mentioned that the return value from a `paginate()` call is an object of a `Pagination` class from Flask-SQLAlchemy? So far, I have used the `items` attribute of this object, which contains the list of items retrieved for the selected page. But this object has a few other attributes that are useful when building pagination links: 361 362 - `has_next`: `True` if there is at least one more page after the current one 363 364 - `has_prev`: `True` if there is at least one more page before the current one 365 366 - `next_num`: page number for the next page 367 368 - `prev_num`: page number for the previous page 369 370 With these four elements, I can generate next and previous page links and pass them down to the templates for rendering: 371 372 *app/routes.py*: Next and previous page links. 373 374 ``` 375 @app.route('/', methods='GET', 'POST') 376 @app.route('/index', methods='GET', 'POST') 377 @login_required 378 def index(): 379 # ... 380 page = request.args.get('page', 1, type=int) 381 posts = db.paginate(current_user.following_posts(), page=page, 382 per_page=app.config'POSTS_PER_PAGE', error_out=False) 383 next_url = url_for('index', page=posts.next_num) \ 384 if posts.has_next else None 385 prev_url = url_for('index', page=posts.prev_num) \ 386 if posts.has_prev else None 387 return render_template('index.html', title='Home', form=form, 388 posts=posts.items, next_url=next_url, 389 prev_url=prev_url) 390 391 @app.route('/explore') 392 @login_required 393 def explore(): 394 page = request.args.get('page', 1, type=int) 395 query = sa.select(Post).order_by(Post.timestamp.desc()) 396 posts = db.paginate(query, page=page, 397 per_page=app.config'POSTS_PER_PAGE', error_out=False) 398 next_url = url_for('explore', page=posts.next_num) \ 399 if posts.has_next else None 400 prev_url = url_for('explore', page=posts.prev_num) \ 401 if posts.has_prev else None 402 return render_template("index.html", title='Explore', posts=posts.items, 403 next_url=next_url, prev_url=prev_url) 404 405 ``` 406 407 The `next_url` and `prev_url` in these two view functions are going to be set to URLs returned by Flask's `url_for()` function, but only if there is a page to go to in that direction. If the current page is at one of the ends of the collection of posts, then the `has_next` or `has_prev` attributes of the `Pagination` object will be `False`, and in that case the link in that direction will be set to `None`. 408 409 One interesting aspect of the `url_for()` function that I haven't discussed before is that you can add any keyword arguments to it, and if the names of those arguments are not part of the URL that is defined for the route, then Flask will include them as query arguments. 410 411 The pagination links are being set to the *index.html* template, so now let's render them on the page, right below the post list: 412 413 *app/templates/index.html*: Render pagination links on the template. 414 415 ``` 416 ... 417 {% for post in posts %} 418 {% include '_post.html' %} 419 {% endfor %} 420 {% if prev_url %} 421 Newer posts 422 {% endif %} 423 {% if next_url %} 424 Older posts 425 {% endif %} 426 ... 427 428 ``` 429 430 This change adds two links below the post list on both the index and explore pages. The first link is labeled "Newer posts", and it points to the previous page (keep in mind I'm showing posts sorted by newest first, so the first page is the one with the newest content). The second link is labeled "Older posts" and points to the next page of posts. If any of these two links is `None`, then it is omitted from the page, through a conditional. 431 432 ## Pagination in the User Profile Page 433 434 The changes for the index page are sufficient for now. However, there is also a list of posts in the user profile page, which shows only posts from the owner of the profile. To be consistent, the user profile page should be changed to match the pagination style of the index page. 435 436 I begin by updating the user profile view function, which still had a list of fake post objects in it. 437 438 *app/routes.py*: Pagination in the user profile view function. 439 440 ``` 441 @app.route('/user/') 442 @login_required 443 def user(username): 444 user = db.first_or_404(sa.select(User).where(User.username == username)) 445 page = request.args.get('page', 1, type=int) 446 query = user.posts.select().order_by(Post.timestamp.desc()) 447 posts = db.paginate(query, page=page, 448 per_page=app.config'POSTS_PER_PAGE', 449 error_out=False) 450 next_url = url_for('user', username=user.username, page=posts.next_num) \ 451 if posts.has_next else None 452 prev_url = url_for('user', username=user.username, page=posts.prev_num) \ 453 if posts.has_prev else None 454 form = EmptyForm() 455 return render_template('user.html', user=user, posts=posts.items, 456 next_url=next_url, prev_url=prev_url, form=form) 457 458 ``` 459 460 To get the list of posts from the user, I take advantage of the fact that the `user.posts` relationship is defined as a write-only relationship, which means that the attribute has a `select()` method that returns a query for the related objects. I take this query and add a `order_by()` clause so that I get the newest posts first, and then do the pagination exactly like I did for the posts in the index and explore pages. Note that the pagination links that are generated by the `url_for()` function in this page need the extra `username` argument, because they are pointing back at the user profile page, which has this username as a dynamic component in the URL. 461 462 Finally, the changes to the *user.html* template are identical to those I made on the index page: 463 464 *app/templates/user.html*: Pagination links in the user profile template. 465 466 ``` 467 ... 468 {% for post in posts %} 469 {% include '_post.html' %} 470 {% endfor %} 471 {% if prev_url %} 472 Newer posts 473 {% endif %} 474 {% if next_url %} 475 Older posts 476 {% endif %} 477 478 ``` 479 480 After you are done experimenting with the pagination feature, you can set the `POSTS_PER_PAGE` configuration item to a more reasonable value: 481 482 *config.py*: Posts per page configuration. 483 484 ``` 485 class Config: 486 # ... 487 POSTS_PER_PAGE = 25 488 489 ``` 490 491 Continue on to the next chapter. 492 493 ## 494 495 --- 496 > [[Flask Mega-Tutorial 学习笔记|返回目录]] 497
